docs.title: Secret Handling in Dotfiles Managers: A Landscape Survey
docs.text: A comparative look at how the mainstream dotfiles / personal configuration tools handle sensitive data — API tokens, SSH keys, credentials, personal identifiers — and the primitives (age, SOPS, git-crypt, password-store, 1Password, etc.) they compose with. The goal is to inform what dodot should offer.

docs.title: 1. Tool-by-tool
docs.text: Each entry uses the same mini-template: identity & philosophy, secret mechanism, UX flow (add / bootstrap / rotate / share), and trade-offs.

docs.title: 1.1 chezmoi
docs.text:
• Identity. Go binary, template-renderer model (not a symlink farm). The source state is a directory whose files are copied/rendered/templated into $HOME on chezmoi apply. Mature and broadly adopted; arguably the reference implementation for dotfiles-with-secrets. Docs: https://www.chezmoi.io.
  • Design philosophy. Declarative destination state expressed through templates (Go text/template + sprig), with first-class template functions for almost every secret backend in existence.
  • Secret mechanism. Two orthogonal strategies, which can be combined:
  • Whole-file encryption at rest. Supports age, GPG, git-crypt, and transcrypt. Files are stored ASCII-armored in the source directory with an encrypted_ filename prefix. See https://www.chezmoi.io/user-guide/encryption/ and https://www.chezmoi.io/user-guide/encryption/age/.
  • Reference-from-vault templates. Template functions pull secrets at apply time from: 1Password, Bitwarden, Dashlane, Keeper, LastPass, KeePassXC, Proton Pass, pass, passhole, gopass, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault, Doppler, ejson, macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager. See https://www.chezmoi.io/user-guide/password-managers/.
  • UX — add a new secret.
  • Whole file: chezmoi add --encrypt ~/.ssh/id_rsa (transparent re-encrypt on chezmoi edit).
  • Templated value: write .zshrc.tmpl containing e.g. export GH_TOKEN={{ onepasswordRead "op://Personal/GitHub/token" }}.
  • UX — bootstrap on a fresh machine. Install chezmoi, then chezmoi init --apply <git-url>. The machine must already have either the age/GPG key present (for encrypted files) or be signed in to the chosen vault (for template-rendered secrets). Bootstrap scripts (run_once_before_*.sh) are commonly used to install and sign in to the password-manager CLI before secrets are referenced.
  • UX — rotate. For encrypted files: chezmoi edit just works. For vault references: rotate in the vault, then chezmoi apply.
  • UX — share public. Vault-reference templates are the killer feature here: the repo contains only pointers (op://...), so it is safe to publish.
  • Trade-offs.
  • Right: broadest backend support in the space by a wide margin; single file can mix encrypted blobs and vault references; declarative; cross-platform (Linux/macOS/Windows).
  • Wrong: template complexity scales rapidly (Go template syntax, not beginner-friendly); vault reference approach requires CLI to be installed and authenticated on every machine, which forks the bootstrap story.

docs.title: 1.2 yadm
docs.text:
• Identity. A thin wrapper over git that treats $HOME directly as the working tree. Bash. Docs: https://yadm.io.
  • Design philosophy. Imperative, git-native. Whatever git does, yadm does — plus a few conventions (alternates, bootstrap hook, encrypt archive).
  • Secret mechanism. Three options, documented side-by-side at https://yadm.io/docs/encryption:
  • Built-in yadm encrypt / yadm decrypt — produces a single GPG- or OpenSSL-encrypted tarball ($HOME/.local/share/yadm/archive) containing everything that matches $HOME/.config/yadm/encrypt patterns. The archive is committed; the plaintext files are git-ignored.
  • git-crypt — yadm git-crypt ... passes through; transparent per-file encryption via .gitattributes filters.
  • transcrypt — same passthrough pattern; symmetric passphrase.
  • UX — add a new secret.
  • Built-in: append /.ssh/*.key to .config/yadm/encrypt, then yadm encrypt, then yadm add .local/share/yadm/archive and commit.
  • git-crypt: yadm git-crypt init, configure .gitattributes, commit.
  • UX — bootstrap. yadm clone <url> followed by yadm decrypt (with the passphrase / GPG key) or yadm git-crypt unlock. Yadm also has a bootstrap hook that runs automatically after clone.
  • UX — rotate. Built-in archive: re-run yadm encrypt, commit new archive. git-crypt: normal git-crypt rekey flow (awkward).
  • UX — share public. Repo is commit-safe with any of the three approaches, though yadm's own docs still recommend a private repo as defence-in-depth.
  • Trade-offs.
  • Right: encryption is native, not bolted on; archive approach is straightforward to understand (one tarball, one passphrase); easy upgrade path from a plain git-managed $HOME.
  • Wrong: archive approach loses per-file history (the archive is opaque to diff); built-in encrypt is GPG/OpenSSL only — no age; no vault integration.

docs.title: 1.3 dotbot
docs.text:
• Identity. Python. Symlink-farm + imperative shell hooks driven by a YAML plan. https://github.com/anishathalye/dotbot.
  • Design philosophy. Deliberately minimal and unopinionated. The tagline is roughly "dotbot does less than you think because git does more than you think." Idempotent plan execution is the core.
  • Secret mechanism. None natively. Secrets are handled by third-party plugins that extend the plan schema:
  • dotbot-age — age encryption of selected files.
  • dotbot-gitcrypt — invokes git-crypt as a plan step. Users frequently just put their encrypted store alongside the dotbot repo and reference it from a shell: step.
  • UX. A typical plan adds a git-crypt: or age: entry; bootstrap is ./install, which runs the plan.
  • Trade-offs.
  • Right: extremely composable; no opinion means no wrong opinion.
  • Wrong: every user re-invents the same bootstrap glue; no standard place for secrets, which means no shared mental model across dotbot repos.

docs.title: 1.4 rcm
docs.text:
• Identity. C/shell, from thoughtbot. Symlink farm with tags and host overrides. https://github.com/thoughtbot/rcm.
  • Secret mechanism. None. rcup, rcdn, mkrc, lsrc are symlink operations only. Users handle secrets entirely out-of-band — typically a separate encrypted store (pass, git-crypt on a private repo) that they source from their rc files.
  • Trade-offs. Simple and stable; offloads the entire problem. No guidance, no answers.

docs.title: 1.5 GNU Stow
docs.text:
• Identity. Perl. The oldest and purest symlink-farm manager. https://www.gnu.org/software/stow/.
  • Secret mechanism. None and by design. Stow only creates symlinks.
  • Typical composition. Users keep secrets either (a) in a private repo stowed alongside the public one, or (b) referenced via pass / 1Password CLI invoked from their shell rc.
  • Trade-offs. Zero opinion, zero help. Whatever you layer on top is your problem.

docs.title: 1.6 home-manager / nix-darwin (via sops-nix or agenix)
docs.text: These are not dotfile managers in the classic sense — they are Nix modules that render a user's environment — but they are the de-facto "declarative dotfiles" for the Nix community, and both mainstream secret modules are worth covering.

docs.title: 1.6.1 sops-nix
docs.text:
• Repo. https://github.com/Mic92/sops-nix. Builds on Mozilla/getsops SOPS.
  • Mechanism. Secrets are stored as SOPS-encrypted files (YAML/JSON/INI/env) in the flake/repo. Encryption backends: age, GPG, AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault. The module decrypts at NixOS activation time (nixos-rebuild switch) using the target machine's SSH host key converted to an age identity, writing plaintext into /run/secrets/<name> with declared owner, group, and permissions. The Nix store itself stays clean.
  • UX — add. Edit .sops.yaml to list recipients (personal age keys + the machine's age-converted SSH host key), then sops secrets.yaml — SOPS opens $EDITOR with plaintext and re-encrypts on save. Commit.
  • UX — bootstrap. The host's SSH host key must already exist (it normally does, since sshd generates it). Personal age key must be present for editing.
  • Trade-offs. Right: only leaf values encrypted — diffs are meaningful; many KMS backends; atomic activation. Wrong: non-trivial setup (.sops.yaml, key conversion); assumes Nix.

docs.title: 1.6.2 agenix
docs.text:
• Repo. https://github.com/ryantm/agenix. A deliberately simpler alternative to sops-nix.
  • Mechanism. Whole-file age encryption only. Recipients are SSH public keys (local, ssh-keyscan'd from running hosts, or pulled from GitHub). Encrypted .age files ship into the Nix store; the module decrypts them at activation using the host's SSH private key and mounts plaintext at /run/agenix/<name>.
  • UX — add. agenix -e secret1.age — decrypts (if present), opens $EDITOR, re-encrypts.
  • Trade-offs. Right: dead-simple; no GPG; reuses existing SSH keys. Wrong: whole-file only (no leaf-value diffs); no MACs on age files, so a hostile committer could corrupt secret contents without detection (sops-nix's explicit pitch for itself).

docs.title: 1.7 git-crypt
docs.text:
• Repo. https://github.com/AGWA/git-crypt. C++.
  • Mechanism. A git clean/smudge filter driven by .gitattributes. Files marked filter=git-crypt diff=git-crypt are AES-256-CTR encrypted on git add, decrypted on checkout, transparently. Keys are shared via GPG (git-crypt add-gpg-user <key-id>) or exported as a symmetric key (git-crypt export-key).
  • UX — add. git-crypt init; edit .gitattributes to mark files (secrets/* filter=git-crypt diff=git-crypt); commit normally.
  • UX — bootstrap. On a new machine: clone, then git-crypt unlock /path/to/symkey or git-crypt unlock with a GPG key already in the keyring.
  • UX — rotate. No real rotation. Adding a new collaborator re-encrypts forward, but the old key stays valid for history.
  • Trade-offs. Right: transparent to git workflow once configured; semantically secure under deterministic chosen-plaintext. Wrong: key rotation is basically "start a new repo"; breaks some git GUIs; filenames and commit messages are not protected.

docs.title: 1.8 transcrypt
docs.text:
• Repo. https://github.com/elasticdog/transcrypt. Bash, OpenSSL-backed.
  • Mechanism. Same clean/smudge-filter approach as git-crypt, but symmetric passphrase only. Each file gets a deterministic per-file salt derived from HMAC-SHA256(filename, password), so identical plaintexts encrypt identically and the filter stays stable across commits.
  • UX. transcrypt to init; transcrypt --add path to mark; clone and transcrypt -c aes-256-cbc -p '<passphrase>' to unlock.
  • Trade-offs. Right: pure Bash, no compile; one passphrase, easy to teach. Wrong: symmetric only — can't revoke a single collaborator; the passphrase is a single point of failure; no multi-recipient model.

docs.title: 1.9 BlackBox
docs.text:
• Repo. https://github.com/StackExchange/blackbox. Archived November 2025 with an explicit "DO NOT USE" notice. Included here for completeness because it shows up in older dotfile write-ups.
  • Mechanism. Per-file GPG, multi-recipient. Admins are added by GPG key ID; any admin can decrypt; blackbox_addadmin re-encrypts all files for the new recipient.
  • Verdict. Abandoned; do not adopt. The design (GPG multi-recipient whole-file) is essentially superseded by SOPS + age.

docs.title: 1.10 Password-store (pass) and gopass
docs.text:
These are not dotfile managers — they are password managers — but they show up constantly as the source that dotfile managers pull from.

  • pass. https://www.passwordstore.org/. One GPG-encrypted file per secret in ~/.password-store, organized by directory. Backed by git for sync. Commands: pass init <gpg-id>, pass insert foo/bar, pass -c foo/bar, pass git push.
  • gopass. https://github.com/gopasspw/gopass. Drop-in compatible; adds age backend, multiple stores, team workflows, Windows support, fuzzy-search, browser integration.
  • Composition with dotfiles. The dotfile repo contains neither secret nor encrypted blob — only a shell snippet like export GH_TOKEN="$(pass show tokens/github)" in .zshrc. Secrets live in a separate (possibly also versioned) password-store.

docs.title: 1.11 SOPS and age as primitives
docs.text:
• age. https://github.com/FiloSottile/age. Minimal file-encryption tool with age1... keys and SSH-key compatibility. "No config options" is its motto. Has become the default for new secret tooling (agenix, sops-nix prefers it, chezmoi recommends it).
  • SOPS. https://github.com/getsops/sops. Mozilla-originated, now a CNCF sandbox project. Operates on structured files (YAML/JSON/INI/env) and encrypts only leaf values, leaving keys and structure in cleartext — so diffs are meaningful. Backends: age, GPG, AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault, Vault, Huawei KMS. Usage: sops edit secrets.yaml, sops -d, sops encrypt -i.
  • Who uses them in the dotfiles space. chezmoi (age, GPG directly — SOPS not natively, but trivial to call via a template output function); dotbot-age; agenix (age only); sops-nix (SOPS). Yadm has not adopted either. Stock git-crypt and transcrypt predate age and use their own primitives.

docs.title: 1.12 1Password / Bitwarden CLI integration patterns
docs.text:
Pattern used by chezmoi, and available to any dotfile manager that can shell out at apply time:

  • User installs op (1Password) or bw (Bitwarden) on the machine and signs in once (often with OS biometrics as the unlock).
  • The dotfile repo contains templates like {{ onepasswordRead "op://Personal/github/token" }} or {{ (bitwarden "item" "example.com").login.password }}.
  • At apply time, the dotfile tool invokes the CLI, gets a fresh value, and writes the rendered file to disk with 0600 perms.
  • Consequence: the repo is fully public-safe; the machine must have a live session with the vault at apply time.

docs.title: 2.1 Encryption-at-rest vs reference-only vs blob-at-apply
docs.text: Approach Examples What's in the repo Decryption requires Transparent encryption in git git-crypt, transcrypt, blackbox Ciphertext, filter config in .gitattributes Key available locally before checkout Encrypted blobs (whole files) chezmoi encrypted_*, yadm archive, agenix, dotbot-age Ciphertext files with a marker Key available before apply Encrypted leaf values SOPS (and sops-nix) Mixed plaintext structure + encrypted values Key available before apply Reference-only / vault pull chezmoi + 1Password/Bitwarden/pass/Vault, gopass-integrated shell configs Placeholders/pointers Authenticated vault session at apply time

docs.title: 2.2 Bootstrap cost on a fresh machine
docs.text:
From cheapest to most expensive:

  1. transcrypt / yadm-archive — one passphrase, typed once. No prior state.
  2. git-crypt with symmetric key — one file, copied in out-of-band.
  3. age / agenix / sops-nix — one key file (or SSH key that's already generated anyway). Portable.
  4. GPG-based anything — requires a GPG identity, keyring, often a hardware token or passphrase. High friction on a brand-new machine.
  5. Vault-reference (1Password / Bitwarden / Vault) — requires install + sign-in + network. Highest cost on day one, lowest cost thereafter because rotation doesn't touch the repo at all.

docs.title: 2.3 Trust root
docs.text: Trust root Tools Passphrase in user's head transcrypt, yadm (OpenSSL/GPG-symmetric), age passphrase mode Local keyfile age, agenix (SSH priv key), chezmoi-age GPG private key (possibly on hardware token) git-crypt, pass, gopass, blackbox, chezmoi-gpg, yadm-GPG Machine SSH host key sops-nix, agenix (for activation-time decryption) Cloud KMS SOPS backends, chezmoi AWS/GCP/Azure/Vault functions Third-party vault session chezmoi with 1Password / Bitwarden / LastPass / Dashlane / Keeper / pass / Vault / Doppler OS keyring chezmoi with macOS Keychain / Windows Credential Manager

docs.title: 2.4 Whole-file vs leaf-value secrets
docs.text:
• Whole-file: git-crypt, transcrypt, yadm archive, agenix, chezmoi encrypted_*, dotbot-age, blackbox. Simplest mental model; diffs are opaque.
  • Leaf-value interpolation: chezmoi templates (with either vault lookup or embedded encrypted maps), SOPS / sops-nix. Diff-friendly, review-friendly, but more moving parts.

Most dotfile secrets (SSH keys, GPG keys, age keys, certificates) are binary-ish and want whole-file encryption. Most config secrets (API tokens, .npmrc, .aws/credentials) want leaf-value interpolation. Good designs accommodate both.

docs.title: 2.5 Team / multi-user vs single-user multi-machine
docs.text:
• Single-user multi-machine (the common dotfiles case) — a symmetric passphrase, a single age keyfile, or a vault with biometric unlock on each machine are all adequate. transcrypt, age, 1Password-style integrations shine here.
  • Team / multi-user — requires multi-recipient encryption (git-crypt GPG mode, SOPS with multiple recipients, blackbox, agenix with multiple SSH keys) or a shared vault. Symmetric schemes (transcrypt) break down because passphrase rotation means a group-wide re-keying event.

docs.title: 3. Synthesis table
docs.text: Tool Mechanism Bootstrap cost Biggest strength Biggest weakness chezmoi (age/GPG) encrypted whole-file blobs keyfile + chezmoi binary transparent edit; same repo mixes encrypted and plaintext age keyfile must reach the machine out-of-band chezmoi (1Password/Bitwarden/Vault) reference-only templates CLI install + signed-in session repo is fully public-safe; rotation free needs live vault at every apply yadm (built-in) GPG/OpenSSL tarball archive passphrase or GPG key one command, one archive opaque diffs, whole-archive rotation yadm + git-crypt transparent git filter GPG key or symkey file git-native git-crypt's own rotation / GUI warts dotbot + plugins whatever the plugin does plugin-dependent flexible no standard — every repo differs rcm — n/a simple no answer for secrets at all GNU Stow — n/a pure symlinks no answer sops-nix SOPS leaf-value encryption SSH host key + personal age key diff-friendly; many KMS backends Nix-only; non-trivial setup agenix age whole-file in Nix store SSH host key very simple, SSH-key-native whole-file only; no MAC git-crypt (standalone) git clean/smudge AES GPG key or symkey transparent git workflow no real rotation story transcrypt (standalone) git filter, symmetric OpenSSL one passphrase Bash-only, zero install symmetric = no revoke blackbox per-file GPG multi-recipient GPG keyring multi-admin sharing abandoned pass / gopass GPG (and age, gopass) per-secret file GPG or age key canonical Unix; composes with anything separate from dotfiles; adds a tool to learn SOPS + age leaf-value encryption, many backends key or KMS access diff-friendly; KMS-friendly not a dotfile tool — you wire it in yourself

docs.title: 4. What the good designs have in common
docs.text:
1. They separate "secret lives here" from "dotfile points at it" whenever possible. The repo either contains encrypted blobs with a published decryption story, or contains only references to a vault. It does not contain plaintext that "you just shouldn't commit." The best tools make the plaintext-in-repo state impossible, not discouraged.
  2. Transparent edit beats explicit encrypt/decrypt. chezmoi edit, agenix -e, sops secrets.yaml, blackbox_edit, and git-crypt's clean/smudge filter all implement the same pattern: the user interacts with plaintext in their $EDITOR, and the tool handles cipher/plaintext transitions. Users do not want to remember to re-encrypt.
  3. Age is winning over GPG for new designs. agenix, sops-nix's preferred mode, chezmoi's recommended default, dotbot-age, gopass's non-default backend — every greenfield tool is picking age. GPG remains only in ecosystems where it's already installed for other reasons (pass, yadm). The lesson: a dotfiles tool that standardises on age as its lowest-friction default and supports "bring your own" (GPG, vault) for people who have them is probably on the right side of this trend.
  4. The 1Password/Bitwarden-CLI pattern is quietly the best UX for single-user multi-machine. Once signed in (often via biometrics), the user never touches a key, a passphrase, or a rotation ceremony. The cost — requiring a vault session at apply time — is usually acceptable. This is what makes chezmoi's template-function approach powerful and worth imitating.
  5. Leaf-value encryption is better for config-style secrets; whole-file for key material. The tools that cover both (chezmoi, SOPS-based stacks) give users the right ergonomics for each case. Tools that only do one (agenix whole-file, yadm archive whole-file) force awkwardness on the other.
  6. Bootstrap is the failure mode. Every secret scheme works fine once it's set up. What distinguishes good designs is how painful the "new laptop, no keys yet" situation is. The best answers in the field are: (a) a single age keyfile you copy from another machine over a USB stick or AirDrop; (b) a vault biometric unlock; (c) an SSH host key that already exists. GPG-based answers are consistently the worst.

docs.title: 5. Implications for dodot
docs.text:
These are observations, not a design spec:

  • Default should be age. It's the low-friction modern primitive and aligns with where the ecosystem is heading.
  • Template/placeholder story matters even if encryption-at-rest is the headline feature. Users will want .npmrc-style leaf-value secrets.
  • Plan for the "bring your own vault" case. 1Password/Bitwarden CLI calls from within rendered files are high-value and relatively simple to support (shell out, capture stdout, write 0600).
  • Do not invent key management. Reuse SSH keys and/or age keys. Do not ship a bespoke keystore.
  • Decide early whether dodot wants to be opinionated (one blessed path, like agenix) or comprehensive (every backend, like chezmoi). The comprehensive path is a long tail of integration work; the opinionated path loses users with existing vault investments. Hybrid: pick one blessed local primitive (age) plus one blessed vault-reference pattern (shell out to a configurable CLI), and stop.
  • Make plaintext-in-repo impossible, not just unrecommended. A dodot add --encrypt workflow that actively refuses to commit plaintext for anything it has seen encrypted once is the kind of ergonomic guard rail the existing tools mostly lack.

docs.title: dodot
docs.text:
A dotfiles manager that adapts to you, rather than the other way around.

Group config files into directories ("packs"). dodot reads filenames as conventions and decides what to do with each file — symlink it, source it from your shell, run it once, install brew formulae. No apply step, no datastore drift, no separate state from git.

docs.title: 1. Start here
docs.text:
• [user/getting-started.lex] — from "I have a dotfiles repo" to your first dodot up.
  • [user/adopting.lex] — migrating an existing $HOME / ~/.config layout into packs.
  • [user/handlers.lex] — the catalog of filename conventions dodot recognizes.

This site is a work in progress. Content is being rolled in piece by piece.

docs.title: 2. Go deeper
docs.text:
• [reference/philosophy.lex] — the design choices behind dodot.
  • [reference/architecture.lex] — how the pieces fit together.
  • [reference/terms-and-concepts.lex] — the full vocabulary.

docs.title: Conditional running
docs.text: For terminology see. For the design rationale see the conditional-running proposal in

docs.title: Conditional Running
docs.text:
Some dotfiles only make sense on certain machines: a Brewfile is macOS-specific; an apt-get-flavoured install.sh is Linux-specific; a Hammerspoon config is dead weight on a Linux laptop. Rather than turning every such file into a Jinja template and rendering empty output on the wrong host, dodot lets you gate files, directories, and whole packs against host facts (OS, arch, hostname) so they only deploy where they apply.

Templates render content that varies between hosts; gates decide whether a file exists on a host. The two compose: a darwin-only template is aliases._darwin.sh.tmpl (gate first, template second).

docs.title: 1. The Five Surfaces, At A Glance
docs.text:
Each surface picks a different granularity. Pick the one that matches what you want to gate.

Five surfaces

Surface Granularity Example filename suffix one file install._darwin.sh directory segment a subtree _darwin/_home/.bashrc [pack] os whole pack [pack] os = ["darwin"] in pack .dodot.toml [mappings.gates] glob (legacy) "install-mac.sh" = "darwin" dodot adopt --only-os adopting in dodot adopt ~/.bashrc --only-os darwin

The first three are the everyday tools. [mappings.gates] is an escape hatch for repos that can't rename files. --only-os is a convenience flag on dodot adopt.

docs.title: 2. Built-In Labels
docs.text:
A label is a token that names a host predicate. dodot ships these labels compiled in — they work zero-config:

Built-in labels

Label Means darwin OS is darwin (macOS) linux OS is linux macos alias for darwin arm64 arch is aarch64 aarch64 same as arm64 x86_64 arch is x86_64

Labels are case-sensitive: _Darwin is unknown and produces a hard error at scan time (typo guard). Stick to the lowercase forms.

docs.title: 3. Filename Suffix: One File, One Predicate
docs.text:
The simplest surface. A ._<label> token sits before the file's extension (or as a trailing segment for extensionless files); on a matching host the suffix is stripped and the file deploys under the cleaned name.

Per-file gates

~/dotfiles/
    mac-tools/
        install._darwin.sh        # only runs on macOS
        install._linux.sh         # only runs on Linux
        Brewfile._darwin          # brew bundle only on macOS
        aliases._darwin.sh        # sourced into shell only on macOS
        home.bashrc._darwin       # → ~/.bashrc on macOS only

On a darwin host, install._darwin.sh is matched by the install handler under its stripped name install.sh and deployed accordingly. On a linux host the same file is gated out and surfaces in dodot status as gated out (darwin).

The suffix composes with file-routing prefixes (the home.X, app.X, xdg.X, lib.X family from ) and with template extensions:

Composition

home.bashrc._darwin               → ~/.bashrc on darwin only
gitconfig.tmpl._darwin            → renders gitconfig only on darwin
aliases._darwin.sh.tmpl           → both: darwin-only AND a template

The reverse nesting (_home/_darwin/... — routing prefix outside, gate inside) is not supported. The symlink resolver owns recursion inside routing-prefix subtrees and is gate-unaware. Put the gate at the outer level: _darwin/_home/....

docs.title: 4. Directory Segment: A Whole Subtree
docs.text:
A _<label>/ directory at the pack root gates everything beneath it. On a matching host the directory expands transparently — its contents surface at the pack root level, with the gate segment stripped from their relative paths. On a mismatch the whole directory is gated out.

Subtree gates

~/dotfiles/
    cross-pack/
        _darwin/
            Brewfile              # only on darwin
            install.sh            # only on darwin
        _linux/
            install.sh            # only on linux
        shared.txt                # always

On macOS, cross-pack/_darwin/Brewfile is treated as if the user had written cross-pack/Brewfile directly — the _darwin/ segment vanishes from the deploy view. On Linux, the entire _darwin/ subtree is gated out.

Gate dirs nest naturally with routing-prefix subtrees and with each other (AND semantics). Gate dirs always sit outside routing prefixes:

Composing with routing prefixes

~/dotfiles/
    mac-tools/
        _darwin/
            _home/
                .hammerspoon/init.lua    # → ~/.hammerspoon/init.lua on darwin
        _arm-mac/
            setup.sh                    # only on darwin AND aarch64

Routing-prefix names (home, xdg, app, lib) cannot be used as gate labels — _home/ is always a routing prefix, never a gate. Other names are looked up in the gate table; unknown labels are a hard error at scan time.

docs.title: 5. Pack-Level: [pack] os
docs.text:
For packs that should only exist on certain operating systems, set [pack] os in the pack's .dodot.toml:

Pack-level OS gating

# mac-tools/.dodot.toml
[pack]
os = ["darwin"]

os is a list of OS identifiers. The canonical value for macOS is "darwin"; "macos" is accepted as an alias. "linux" is the canonical value on Linux. Set to ["darwin", "linux"] for "either." Empty or absent means "all OSes" (today's behaviour).

Note: template variables expose dodot.os as "macos" on macOS, but gate OS matching uses "darwin" as the canonical form and accepts "macos" as an alias — the two surfaces are not identical.

On a non-matching host, the entire pack is short-circuited at scan time — no preprocessing fires, no handlers run, no symlinks land. dodot status surfaces the pack under an "Inactive on this OS" section so you know it's there but skipped:

Status output (running on linux)

$ dodot status
  shared-tools/
    vimrc                  ➞ ~/.config/vim/vimrc       deployed
    …
  Inactive on this OS
    mac-tools (os=darwin, current=linux)

Root-level [pack] os is rejected (gating every pack from the root would silently neutralise the dotfiles repo for hosts not in the list — almost always a misconfiguration). Set it per-pack.

docs.title: 6. User-Defined Labels: [gates]
docs.text:
Need a label not in the built-in seed? Define one in `.dodot.toml`

Each entry maps a label name to a table of (dimension, value) equality checks AND-ed together. Recognised dimensions: os, arch, hostname, username — same set templates expose under dodot.*.

Once defined, labels work in the filename-grammar and glob-table surfaces — filename suffix (._<label>), directory segment (_<label>/), and [mappings.gates]:

Using a custom label

~/dotfiles/
    shell/
        aliases._laptop.sh        # only on the laptop
    apple-silicon/
        _arm-mac/
            install.sh            # only on darwin + aarch64

Note: [pack] os accepts OS identifier strings only ("darwin", "linux", …), not custom gate labels. dodot adopt --only-os accepts custom labels but validates them against the root config's gate table only — a label defined solely in a pack .dodot.toml is not visible there (see §8 for the workaround).

Constraints on label names: they must match [A-Za-z0-9_-]+ (so they can be parsed from filenames and directories) and must not collide with routing-prefix tokens (home/xdg/app/lib). Both are hard errors at config load.

Labels stack across the standard config layers (compiled defaults \< root .dodot.toml \< pack .dodot.toml): a pack can introduce new labels without repeating root labels. A pack that redefines a label from root or the built-in seed replaces that predicate entirely — dimensions are not merged across layers.

docs.title: 7. Glob Escape Hatch: [mappings.gates]
docs.text:
For repos where renaming files isn't an option (or where the gate is a property of an external project's filename), use [mappings.gates]:

Glob → label

[mappings.gates]
"install-mac.sh" = "darwin"
"Brewfile"       = "darwin"

Patterns match against the same top-level pack entries the scanner surfaces. Globs containing path separators ("setup/*.sh") only match if a top-level entry has that shape — the symlink handler's nested per-file recursion is intentionally gate-unaware (same posture as gates inside routing-prefix subtrees).

A file carrying both a filename gate (._<label>) and a matching [mappings.gates] entry is a hard error: pick one source of truth. Invalid glob patterns are also a hard error at scan time (no silent typos).

docs.title: 8. Adopting With A Gate: dodot adopt --only-os
docs.text:
dodot adopt defaults to "no gate" — adopting ~/.bashrc from a darwin host produces home.bashrc (no suffix), so re-deploying on any host puts the file back at ~/.bashrc. Pass `--only-os \<label>` to wrap the adopted entry in a gate dir so re-deploy only fires on matching hosts:

Gated adopt

$ dodot adopt ~/.bashrc --only-os darwin --into shell

The pack tree ends up as

The label is validated against the gate table at the start of the command, so an unknown label fails before any filesystem work happens. The validation reads the root .dodot.toml's [gates] only (plus the built-in seed); labels defined exclusively in a pack's .dodot.toml are not visible here. If you need a custom label for --only-os, define it in the root config — it can still be referenced from any pack via filename grammar or [mappings.gates] afterwards.

docs.title: 9. When To Use Gates Versus Templates
docs.text:
Both gates and templates handle "this varies between hosts," but they answer different questions:

Gates vs templates

Question Tool Should this file exist on this host? gate Should this directory deploy on this host? gate Should this whole pack run on this host? gate What does this line look like on this host? template What value goes in this {{ }} slot? template

Reach for a template when the file always exists but its content varies between hosts. Reach for a gate when the question is binary — deploy or don't.

The two compose: aliases._darwin.sh.tmpl is darwin-only AND a template. The gate runs first (drops on Linux, no template render fires for the gated-out file). The template runs second (renders aliases.sh on darwin, which the shell handler picks up).

docs.title: 10. Reading Gate Status Output
docs.text:
dodot status surfaces every gate decision so you don't have to guess what the host did:

Status output (running on darwin)

$ dodot status
  mac-tools/
    install.sh           ×  run script     never run
    install._linux.sh    ·  not deployed   gated out (linux) [1]
    Brewfile             ⚙  brew install   not installed
  Inactive on this OS
    linux-tools (os=linux, current=darwin)

Errors:
  [1] expected os=linux; got os=darwin

The footnote shows the predicate the gate expected and what the host actually has. For passing-gate files the row is rendered under the stripped name (install.sh not install._darwin.sh) because that's what dodot will deploy. For failing gates the original on-disk name is shown so you can find the file.

docs.title: 11. Limitations
docs.text:
Things gates intentionally do not do:

  • No predicate language. Labels are equality-only AND conjunctions. For OR, define multiple labels and use multiple filenames.
  • No filename stacking. install._darwin._arm64.sh is not parsed as "darwin AND arm64." Use a compound user-defined label (arm-mac = { os = "darwin", arch = "aarch64" }) and write install._arm-mac.sh.
  • No negation. There's no _!darwin syntax. Write the positive form for the OSes you do want.
  • No nested gates inside routing-prefix subtrees. _home/_darwin/... is not recognised — the symlink handler owns recursion inside routing prefixes. Put the gate at the outer level: _darwin/_home/....
  • No profile selection. dodot is single-config-per-machine; hostname-based gates are the closest analog. See §7.

docs.title: 12. Diagnostic Tips
docs.text: - dodot status is the source of truth — it shows every gated file under its actual disposition. - dodot config prints the fully resolved config including the [gates] table after merging. Useful when a label appears not to fire. - Unknown gate labels are a scan-time error, not a silent skip. A typo in _darwn.sh or in a [mappings.gates] entry like "foo.sh" = "darwn" stops dodot up with a clear "unknown gate label" diagnostic.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
One of dodot's principles is to minimize both adoption and migration cost (off dodot into the next dotfiles tool). One way it does that is by using the dotfiles themselves as the description of what to do: files grouped into packs, and filenames the scanner can infer behavior from. dodot is convention-based — given a name, it knows what to do with the file. Adding bin/ directories to $PATH, running brew against Brewfile, sourcing aliases.sh at shell startup — all of it falls out of names you would pick anyway.

The convention is rooted in common usage patterns, so for most people the default layout is the most natural approach, and is useful in its own right beyond dodot.

When the convention doesn't fit, you can customize. For path-related behavior, most knobs are reachable two ways: a .dodot.toml entry, or a filename / directory naming convention like _home/ or home.X. The two surfaces serve two preferences — a cleaner file tree with the choices captured in .dodot.toml, or an explicit setup where the names themselves carry the intent and no extra config file is needed.

Scope is the other axis. Filesystem naming acts per file. .dodot.toml acts per pack, or repo-wide from the root config — so when many files need the same adjustment a config entry is usually the cleanest path.

dodot ships a small set of CLI helpers for working with config

$ dodot config list                  # show resolved values
$ dodot config get <key>             # show one key with its inline doc
$ dodot config set <key> <value>
$ dodot config unset <key>
$ dodot config gen > .dodot.toml     # write a commented starter file

docs.title: 1. Where Configuration Lives
docs.text:
Configuration is loaded from .dodot.toml files in two locations:

  • Root config: $DOTFILES_ROOT/.dodot.toml. Applies to every pack.
  • Pack config: $DOTFILES_ROOT/<pack>/.dodot.toml. Applies to that pack only.

Both are optional. Every key has a compiled-in default; you only put into .dodot.toml the values you want to override. Pack configuration layers on top of root configuration, so you can set a sensible default at the root and override it per pack.

Merge rules:

  • Scalars and arrays: override (the later-layer value replaces the earlier one, no accumulation).
  • Maps: deep-merge (nested keys combine across layers, but any scalar or array within still overrides).

Some sections are root-only — they're read from the root .dodot.toml and per-pack overrides are ignored. [secret] and [profiling] fall in this bucket; [pack] os is the mirror image (pack-only — root-level entries are rejected).

docs.title: 2. The [pack] Section
docs.text:
Controls pack-level behavior.

Pack ignore patterns

[pack]
ignore = [
    ".git",
    ".svn",
    ".hg",
    "node_modules",
    ".DS_Store",
    "*.swp",
    "*~",
    "#*#",
    ".env*",
    ".terraform"
]

ignore is glob patterns that dodot skips during pack discovery and file scanning. Matching files are not considered for any handler. The defaults cover version-control noise, editor swapfiles, and a few directories that are notoriously never meant to be deployed.

Note: to skip an entire pack, drop a .dodotignore marker file in that pack's directory (the "pack-ignore" mechanism). [pack] ignore is for patterns within a pack.

docs.title: 2.1. os
docs.text:
OS allowlist for the pack. When set, the whole pack is short-circuited at scan time on hosts whose OS isn't in the list — no preprocessing, no handlers, no symlinks. Inactive packs surface in dodot status under "Inactive on this OS" rather than disappearing silently.

Pack-level OS gating

[pack]
os = ["darwin"]

The canonical OS value for macOS is "darwin"; "macos" is accepted as an alias that resolves to "darwin". On Linux the canonical value is "linux". Empty or absent means "all OSes" (today's default). Note: template variables expose dodot.os as "macos" on macOS — gate OS values and template OS values are not the same surface. See §5.

Only meaningful at pack level — root-level [pack] os is a configuration error (it would gate every pack against the current host, almost always unintended).

docs.title: 3. The [symlink] Section
docs.text:
Controls how the symlink handler resolves targets. Full path-resolution rules live in; this section is the config knobs.

By default, every pack-root entry deploys to $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/<pack>/<name> (so nvim/init.lua → ~/.config/nvim/init.lua). The escape hatches come in pairs — a config list and a per-file naming convention:

  • force_home / home.X filename / _home/ directory → $HOME/.<name>
  • force_app / app.X filename / _app/ directory → ~/Library/Application Support/<name> (macOS; falls back to XDG on Linux or when app_uses_library = false)
  • xdg.X filename / _xdg/ directory → escape from a routing override back to plain XDG
  • lib.X filename / _lib/ directory → ~/Library/<name> (macOS only)

docs.title: 3.1. force_home
docs.text:
Files that must land in $HOME/.<name> regardless of the default XDG rule. These are decades-old conventions that precede XDG and are hardcoded by other tools (shell init, ssh interop, etc.).

Force home

[symlink]
force_home = [
    "ssh",            # .ssh/ - security critical
    "aws",            # .aws/ - credentials
    "kube",           # .kube/ - kubernetes config
    "bashrc",         # .bashrc - shell expects in $HOME
    "zshrc",          # .zshrc
    "profile",        # .profile
    "bash_profile",
    "bash_login",
    "bash_logout",
    "inputrc"         # readline config
]

Override to add your own entries or remove ones you don't need. Equivalent per-file: prefix the filename with home. or place it under _home/.

docs.title: 3.2. force_app
docs.text:
macOS: GUI-app folder names that route a pack-root entry to ~/Library/Application Support/<name>/<rest> automatically, without needing the _app/ directory prefix. Matching is case-sensitive (Library folder names are case-sensitive on macOS) and only against the first path segment.

Force app

[symlink]
force_app = ["Code", "Cursor", "Zed", "Emacs"]

On Linux (or on macOS with app_uses_library = false), app_support_dir collapses to $XDG_CONFIG_HOME — but force_app is still applied. So force_app = ["Code"] on Linux routes Code/init.json to $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/Code/init.json (skipping the per-pack namespace), not to the macOS Application Support root. If you don't want that behavior on Linux, drop the entry from the list there (or override per-pack).

The shipped defaults stay under a 100-entry budget — that's a build-time invariant on force_app's seed, not a user validation. You can set a longer list yourself; it just won't be a sane thing to do.

Equivalent per-file: prefix with app. or place under _app/.

docs.title: 3.3. app_aliases
docs.text:
macOS: pack-name → app-folder rewrites. When a pack's name matches a key, every entry in that pack reroutes from the default <xdg_config_home>/<pack>/<rest> to <app_support_dir>/<value>/<rest>. Useful when your pack directory and the app's Library folder have different names.

App aliases

[symlink.app_aliases]
"vscode"        = "Code"
"cursor-editor" = "Cursor"

Defaults empty. Same Linux fallback as force_app.

docs.title: 3.4. app_uses_library
docs.text:
macOS toggle. When true (the default), _app/ directories, app.X filenames, force_app, and app_aliases route through ~/Library/Application Support. When false, they all collapse to plain ~/.config (Linux-style placement). _lib/ and lib.X are unaffected — those explicitly target ~/Library/.

App-uses-library toggle

[symlink]
app_uses_library = false   # opt out of Application Support routing on this Mac

Ignored on non-macOS hosts (the resolver collapses app_support_dir to xdg_config_home everywhere except macOS).

docs.title: 3.5. protected_paths
docs.text:
Files dodot refuses to symlink by default, because doing so is almost always a mistake. Private SSH keys, GPG state, cloud credentials.

Protected paths

[symlink]
protected_paths = [
    ".ssh/id_rsa",
    ".ssh/id_ed25519",
    ".ssh/id_dsa",
    ".ssh/id_ecdsa",
    ".ssh/authorized_keys",
    ".gnupg",
    ".aws/credentials",
    ".password-store",
    ".config/gh/hosts.yml",
    ".kube/config",
    ".docker/config.json"
]

Remove an entry to allow dodot to symlink it anyway.

docs.title: 3.6. targets
docs.text:
Per-file symlink target overrides. Maps a pack-relative filename to an absolute or relative target path. Absolute paths are used as-is; relative paths resolve against $XDG_CONFIG_HOME.

Targets

[symlink.targets]
"mysterious.conf" = "/var/etc/mysterious.conf"
"home-bound.conf" = "my-documents/home-bound.conf"

docs.title: 3.7. plist_extensions
docs.text:
Filename suffixes (without leading dot) that `dodot git-install-filters` and the .gitattributes writer treat as plists. Some apps store plists with non-standard suffixes (.binplist, .savedState); register the extra extensions here to flow them through the same clean/smudge pipeline.

Plist extensions

[symlink]
plist_extensions = ["plist", "binplist"]

Default ["plist"]. Comparison is case-insensitive, and the list honors the standard root → pack inheritance.

docs.title: 4. The [path] Section
docs.text: Settings for the PATH handler (the one that adds bin/ directories to $PATH).

docs.title: 4.1. auto_chmod_exec
docs.text:
Whether dodot up automatically adds the execute bit (+x) to files inside bin/ directories. Default true.

Auto-chmod

[path]
auto_chmod_exec = true

Files in a bin/ directory are there because they're meant to be commands on $PATH, but the execute bit gets lost easily — git on macOS defaults to core.fileMode = false, and manual file creation often forgets chmod +x. Without +x the shell finds the file via PATH lookup but fails with "permission denied", a confusing error when the file is clearly in the right place.

Files that are already executable are left untouched; failures are reported as warnings rather than hard errors. Set to false if you have bin/ files that intentionally should not be executable (data files, sourced library scripts).

docs.title: 5. The [mappings] Section
docs.text:
Overrides the default filename-to-handler map. Each key is a handler name; each value is either a single pattern or a list of patterns.

Mappings

[mappings]
path = "bin"
install = ["install.sh", "install.bash", "install.zsh"]
shell = ["*.sh", "*.bash", "*.zsh"]
homebrew = "Brewfile"
ignore = []
skip = ["README", "README.*", "LICENSE", "LICENSE.*", "CHANGELOG", "CHANGELOG.*", "CONTRIBUTING", "CONTRIBUTING.*", "AUTHORS", "AUTHORS.*", "NOTICE", "NOTICE.*", "COPYING", "COPYING.*"]

The shell wildcards match at depth-1 only — any .sh/.bash/.zsh file at the pack's root is sourced. A .sh script tucked inside a subdirectory of the pack (for example hypr/scripts/foo.sh) is not pulled in; nested files flow through the symlink handler the same way every other nested file does. That carve-out is what keeps window-manager and tmux helper scripts (which live at ~/.config/<app>/scripts/*.sh and are invoked by other tools, not the shell) from being silently sourced into your login shell.

Shell extensions (.sh, .bash, .zsh) carry real meaning in dodot. For install, the extension selects the interpreter that runs the script: .sh and .bash run under bash, .zsh runs under zsh. For shell, the files are sourced into whatever shell reads dodot-init.sh — put zsh-only syntax in .zsh, bash-only syntax in .bash, and portable snippets in .sh. The user's login shell does not affect which install.* interpreter is picked; the extension is the contract.

install is list-only: even a single install script must be written as a TOML array (install = ["install.sh"]). The older single-string form (install = "install.sh") no longer parses — update any older configs that use it.

Two of the keys map to filter handlers — real handlers that claim a match but produce no executable intent. Their job is to keep matching files away from the deploying handlers (precise mappings, catchall symlink):

  • ignore — claims matches and drops them silently, mirroring .gitignore. Nothing surfaces in dodot status. Priority 100.
  • skip — claims matches and surfaces them in dodot status as skipped, but does not deploy them. Defaults cover the documentation/legal files (README, LICENSE, CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING, AUTHORS, NOTICE, COPYING and their .* variants), matched case-insensitively. Override per-pack with skip = [] to deploy a README intentionally. Priority 50.

install sits at priority 20, above the priority-10 shell wildcard, so as long as install.sh is in mappings.install (the default) it routes to the install handler rather than being claimed by the shell glob — the install hook never gets accidentally sourced. The other precise mappings (shell, path, homebrew) sit at priority 10; the catchall symlink at priority 0. So a file the user said to drop is dropped, full stop — ignore over skip over install over the rest of the precise mappings over catchall. (If you override mappings.install to drop install.sh, the shell wildcard will claim it — that's the user's choice.)

Distinct from [pack] ignore: [mappings] ignore/skip apply only to handler dispatch within a known pack, while [pack] ignore affects pack discovery and scanning. To skip an entire pack, drop a .dodotignore marker file (the "pack-ignore" mechanism).

docs.title: 5.1. [mappings.gates]
docs.text:
Glob → gate-label map for repos that can't rename files. Each entry says "this glob inherits this gate"; on a non-matching host the file is dropped (same effect as a filename suffix).

Glob-based gating

[mappings.gates]
"install-mac.sh" = "darwin"
"Brewfile"       = "darwin"

Patterns match the top-level pack entries the scanner surfaces. A file carrying both a filename gate (._<label>) and a matching [mappings.gates] entry is a hard error — pick one source of truth. Invalid glob patterns are also a hard error at scan time. See §7.

docs.title: 6. The [gates] Section
docs.text:
User-defined gate labels. Each entry maps a label name to a table of (dimension, value) equality checks AND-ed together. Gates can then be referenced from filename suffixes (install._<label>.sh), directory segments (_<label>/), and the [mappings.gates] map.

User-defined labels

[gates]
laptop  = { hostname = "mbp-arthur" }
work    = { hostname = "work-laptop" }
arm-mac = { os = "darwin", arch = "aarch64" }

Dimensions: os, arch, hostname, username — same set templates expose under dodot.*. Label names must match [A-Za-z0-9_-]+ and must not collide with routing-prefix tokens (home/xdg/app/lib); both rules are hard errors at config load.

User entries extend the built-in seed (darwin, linux, macos, arm64, aarch64, x86_64). Redefining an existing built-in label (e.g. darwin = { … } in [gates]) replaces its predicate entirely — it does not merge dimensions into the built-in. Shadowing a built-in is allowed but unusual; most user labels use names the seed doesn't claim. For the full surface and composition rules, see.

docs.title: 7. The [preprocessor] Section
docs.text: Controls the preprocessing pipeline. For the concept, see.

docs.title: 7.1. Global kill switch
docs.text:
Global preprocessor toggle

[preprocessor]
enabled = true

Set to false to disable all preprocessors. .tmpl files (and other preprocessor-matched files) will deploy verbatim.

docs.title: 7.2. [preprocessor.template]
docs.text:
Template engine configuration.

Template configuration

[preprocessor.template]
extensions = ["tmpl", "template"]
no_reverse = ["complex-config.toml.tmpl", "*.gen.tmpl"]

[preprocessor.template.vars]
editor    = "nvim"
host_tier = "workstation"

extensions is the list of trigger extensions. Both ".j2" and "j2" are tolerated (leading dot optional).

[preprocessor.template.vars] defines variables available in templates under their bare names. See for usage. Reserved: dodot and env are built-in namespaces, so using them as var names is a hard error at load time.

no_reverse is glob patterns (matched against the source file's basename) whose reverse-merge in dodot transform check is bypassed. Templates listed here still render normally on dodot up and stay in the divergence cache; they just skip the heuristic that tries to backport changes from the deployed copy into the source. Useful for templates that are mostly dynamic — the heuristic degrades there and produces more conflict markers than usable diffs.

docs.title: 7.3. [preprocessor.age]
docs.text:
Opt-in *.age whole-file decryption. Off by default so a fresh dodot install never shells out to age against random files.

Age decryption

[preprocessor.age]
enabled    = true
extensions = ["age"]
identity   = ""    # empty: defer to $AGE_IDENTITY, then ~/.config/age/identity.txt

extensions matches the same shape as template.extensions; add entries when your repo uses non-standard suffixes (e.g. ["age", "age.txt"]). identity is the path to the age identity file used for decryption; leaving it empty defers to the runtime, which checks $AGE_IDENTITY first, then falls back to ~/.config/age/identity.txt (the conventional age-keygen destination).

Do not add asc to extensions unless your repo only stores ASCII-armored encrypted payloads under that suffix. .asc is conventionally used for armored public keys and detached signatures (release signatures, package-manager keys), neither of which gpg will decrypt; routing them through gpg --decrypt produces confusing failures.

docs.title: 7.4. [preprocessor.gpg]
docs.text:
Opt-in *.gpg whole-file decryption. Same posture as age. gpg-agent supplies the identity, so there's no identity field — auth is your existing gpg setup, not dodot's job to configure.

Gpg decryption

[preprocessor.gpg]
enabled    = true
extensions = ["gpg"]

docs.title: 8. The [profiling] Section
docs.text:
Root-only. Controls shell-init timing instrumentation. Per-pack overrides are ignored — the init script is one thing, you can't half-profile it.

Profiling configuration

[profiling]
enabled        = true
keep_last_runs = 100

When enabled = true (the default), the generated dodot-init.sh carries a timing wrapper around each source and PATH line. bash 5+ / zsh sessions emit one TSV per shell startup under <data_dir>/probes/shell-init/; older shells fall through to the no-op path even with the wrapper present. Set enabled = false to make the init script byte-identical to the pre-Phase-2 form.

keep_last_runs caps retained TSV files; older ones get pruned at the end of every dodot up. At ~4 KB per run, the default budget is roughly 400 KB on disk.

docs.title: 9. The [secret] Section
docs.text:
Root-only. Configuration for secret resolution in templates (the secret(...) template function). Per-pack overrides are ignored — secret tooling (binaries, env vars like $PASSWORD_STORE_DIR, OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN) is a property of the host, not of any individual pack.

All providers default to enabled = false. Templates that call secret(...) against an unregistered scheme surface a "no provider for scheme" render error at dodot up time.

docs.title: 9.1. Global kill switch
docs.text:
Master switch

[secret]
enabled = true

Default true. Setting enabled = false disables the entire secret subsystem without removing per-provider blocks.

docs.title: 9.2. [secret.providers]
docs.text:
One block per supported provider. Each is opt-in; flip enabled = true to register the scheme.

Providers

[secret.providers.pass]
enabled   = true
store_dir = ""    # optional: overrides $PASSWORD_STORE_DIR

[secret.providers.op]
enabled = true    # 1Password CLI, scheme `op://`

[secret.providers.bw]
enabled = false   # Bitwarden CLI, scheme `bw:`

[secret.providers.sops]
enabled = false   # Mozilla SOPS, scheme `sops:`

[secret.providers.keychain]
enabled = false   # macOS Keychain, scheme `keychain:` (macOS only)

[secret.providers.secret_tool]
enabled = false   # freedesktop Secret Service, scheme `secret-tool:` (Linux-first)

The TOML key is secret_tool (underscore), but the scheme prefix in secret(...) calls is secret-tool: (hyphen, matching the binary name). Error messages translate between the two so a "no provider for scheme secret-tool" hint points at the right [secret.providers.secret_tool] block.

pass.store_dir overrides $PASSWORD_STORE_DIR for the pass provider. Empty (the default) leaves dodot reading the env var, which itself falls back to $HOME/.password-store.

For details on schemes, providers, and the secret(...) template function, see.

docs.title: 10. Inheritance Model
docs.text:
Most sections follow the same three-layer model: compiled defaults, then root .dodot.toml, then pack .dodot.toml. The outermost layer that sets a key wins for scalars and arrays; for maps, the layers deep-merge. The exceptions: [secret] and [profiling] are root-only (per-pack entries are ignored), and [pack] os is pack-only (root-level entries are rejected).

Example: you set [preprocessor.template.vars] editor = "nvim" at the root. In a pack for work configs, you set [preprocessor.template.vars] editor = "vscode". That pack renders templates with editor = "vscode"; all others render with editor = "nvim". All other keys under [preprocessor.template] (enabled, extensions) remain as defined at the root.

To see the fully resolved configuration for a context, run dodot config list. To inspect a single key with its inline doc, run dodot config get <key>.

docs.title: Filters & ignores — keeping files out of dodot's way
docs.text:
By default, dodot processes every directory in your dotfiles root and dispatches every source file inside to a handler. Sometimes that's not what you want — a directory of notes, a *.bak file, a README that should ship in your repo but not deploy. This doc surveys the five mechanisms for keeping files out of dodot's way, and when to reach for each.

Two layers, five mechanisms

``` note :: Terminology — this doc uses [pack]

  • Discovery layer — stop dodot from looking at the file at all (.dodotignore, [pack] ignore).
  • Dispatch layer — let dodot see the file but route it to a filter handler ([mappings] ignore, [mappings] skip, gates). 
    ## 1. When you reach for this doc

- You have a directory in your dotfiles repo that isn't a real pack (notes, scratch, work-in-progress). 
- You have files inside a pack that shouldn't deploy (READMEs, `*.bak`, `TODO.md`, build artifacts). 
- You want a file visible in `dodot status` so you remember it exists, but not deployed. 
- You want a file deployed only on certain hosts (macOS-only, ARM-only, hostname-specific) — that's a **gate**, covered in detail [at](./conditional-running.lex) but its relationship to filters is summarized here. 

## 2. The mental model

<!-- lex:table align=lll -->

<!-- /lex:table -->

**Two questions decide which mechanism fits**

The two pack-pattern ignores look similar but differ in **layer**: `[pack] ignore` stops file scanning inside a pack at discovery time; `[mappings] ignore` lets the file be discovered, then claims it at handler dispatch. In practice the difference rarely matters. Reach for `[pack] ignore` for repo-wide noise (`*.swp`, `.DS_Store`); reach for `[mappings] ignore` for the occasional one-off in a single pack.

## 3. Whole pack invisible — `.dodotignore`

<!-- lex:shell -->

<!-- /lex:shell -->

### A marker file inside a directory tells dodot to skip that directory as a pack. Pure file-presence check; the contents are never read.

\# In your dotfiles root: $ touch notes/.dodotignore $ dodot list ; notes/ no longer appears

**Or, equivalently, the dedicated command**

``` shell

dodot addignore notes

Useful for directories that live in your repo but aren't meant to be deployed: scratch space, notes, README-only directories, half-migrated state, packs you've turned off but don't want to delete.

To reverse: rm <dir>/.dodotignore. dodot doesn't ship an addignore --remove; deletion is by hand on purpose, matching the "git is your history" posture.

Critical sequencing: if the pack was previously deployed, run dodot down <pack> first, then add the marker. up and down only walk discovered packs — adding the marker first hides the pack from discovery and leaves the deployed symlinks behind.

docs.title: 4. Whole pattern invisible at scan time — [pack] ignore
docs.text:
A list of glob patterns that pack discovery skips entirely. Defaults cover version-control noise, editor swapfiles, and common build artifacts

[pack]
ignore = [
    ".git", ".svn", ".hg",
    "node_modules", ".DS_Store",
    "*.swp", "*~", "#*#",
    ".env*", ".terraform",
]

Override the default list to add or remove patterns. Setting [pack] ignore = [...] replaces the defaults — re-list the standard noise alongside your additions if you want to keep them.

[pack] ignore is the broadest in-pack hammer. Everything below operates on files dodot has already discovered.

docs.title: 5. Silent drop at dispatch — [mappings] ignore
docs.text:
Glob patterns that drop matched files silently from handler dispatch. No entry in `dodot status`. Same mental model as `.gitignore`

[mappings]
ignore = ["*.bak", "scratch.txt"]

The defaults are empty — common noise is handled by [pack] ignore one layer earlier.

Reach for [mappings] ignore when:

  • You want a per-pack drop list (set in <pack>/.dodot.toml) without touching the wider [pack] ignore.
  • You want the file dropped at dispatch but still discovered (rare, but useful when a separate tool reads the file).

docs.title: 6. Visible drop — [mappings] skip
docs.text:
Same shape as `[mappings] ignore`, but matched files surface in `dodot status` as `skipped`. The defaults cover common docs/legal files

[mappings]
skip = [
    "README", "README.*",
    "LICENSE", "LICENSE.*",
    "CHANGELOG", "CHANGELOG.*",
    "CONTRIBUTING", "CONTRIBUTING.*",
    "AUTHORS", "AUTHORS.*",
    "NOTICE", "NOTICE.*",
    "COPYING", "COPYING.*",
]

Matched case-insensitively against the basename.

Override per-pack to deploy a `README` intentionally

# in <pack>/.dodot.toml
[mappings]
skip = []

Or replace with your own list

[mappings]
skip = ["TODO.md", "DESIGN.md"]

skip is the right answer when you want to see in status that a file exists and was deliberately not deployed. ignore is the right answer when you want the file invisible.

docs.title: 7. Conditional drop — gates
docs.text:
A gate drops a file only when a host predicate doesn't match. Examples: install._darwin.sh is dropped on Linux, anything inside _darwin/ is dropped on Linux, anything in a pack with [pack] os = ["darwin"] is dropped on Linux. In dodot status these surface as gated out (<label>) with the expected vs actual host facts.

Gates compose with the rest of the filters: a gated-out file is treated as filtered (no executable intent, status visible).

The full surface — filename suffix ._<label>, directory segment _<label>/, [pack] os, [mappings.gates] glob escape hatch — lives at. This doc only flags the relationship: gates are a filter, but they're conditional on the host rather than unconditional.

docs.title: 8. Order of evaluation
docs.text:
When more than one filter could match a file:

  • .dodotignore short-circuits everything below — the directory isn't scanned at all.
  • [pack] ignore runs at scan time, before handler dispatch — matched files never reach the dispatch layer.
  • At dispatch, ignore wins over skip (silent-drop is the stronger signal).
  • Both ignore and skip win over precise mappings (shell, install, …) and the catch-all symlink.
  • Gates run at scan time alongside [pack] ignore; a gated-out file behaves like skip in status, but for a host-conditional reason.

For the priority numbers and rule-matcher details, see.

docs.title: 9. Watch out for
docs.text:
• Override replaces, doesn't merge. Setting [mappings] skip = ["TODO.md"] drops the README/LICENSE defaults from that scope. Re-list the defaults if you want to keep them.
  • .dodotignore after down, not before. Adding the marker to a previously-deployed pack hides it from discovery; up and down only reconcile discovered packs. Run dodot down <pack> first, then add the marker — otherwise the deployed symlinks stay live.
  • [mappings] ignore is per-pack-or-root. Like every [mappings] key, it's set in .dodot.toml and replaces (not merges) at the pack level. Pack-level setting wins for that pack; root-level applies to every pack that doesn't override.
  • protected_paths is separate. dodot also refuses to symlink a curated list of security-sensitive paths (SSH private keys, .gnupg, AWS credentials, …). That's enforced by the symlink handler, not the filter layer — see §6.

docs.title: 10. Live edits
docs.text:
Filter and gate decisions are recomputed on every dodot up, dodot status, and dodot down. There's no persisted "filter state" — edit [mappings] ignore, [mappings] skip, [mappings.gates], or [pack] ignore, and the change takes effect on the next dodot invocation.

For [mappings] filters, dodot up reconciles per-pack state on every run, so adding *.bak to [mappings] ignore cleans up any previously-deployed *.bak symlinks for that pack on the next up.

.dodotignore is the exception (see §9). The marker hides discovery; reconciliation walks discovery; so down before adding the marker is the safe sequence.

docs.title: 11. See also
docs.text:
• — the same material from the handler side, with dispatch-layer details.
  • — the priority ladder and config shape for [mappings].
  • — gates in full.
  • — the shortcut for dropping .dodotignore.
  • §6 — protected_paths, the security-flavored sibling of these filters.
  • . ,,

docs.title: Paths
docs.text: Terminology — this doc uses [pack], [handler], and [dotfiles root]. If any of those are unfamiliar, see.

docs.title: Paths
docs.text:
When you run dodot up, every file in your packs lands somewhere on disk. This document is about the somewhere — how dodot decides, what the defaults give you for free, and what knobs you reach for when the defaults aren't right.

The short version: dodot deploys to $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/<pack>/... by default. The handful of files that don't belong there — Unix-tradition holdouts like .ssh and .bashrc, macOS GUI app config, the occasional one-off custom path — have explicit escape hatches that read at a glance.

docs.title: 1. When you reach for this doc
docs.text:
• You ran dodot status and a file landed somewhere unexpected. You want to understand why.
  • You have a Unix-tradition file (.bashrc, .ssh/config) that needs to be at ~/.X rather than under ~/.config/.
  • You have a directory of files that should not have your pack's name in their deployed path.
  • You're adding a macOS pack and need to deploy to ~/Library/Application Support/.
  • You need one file at a fully custom absolute path (/etc/..., /opt/...).

docs.title: 2. The three roots
docs.text:
Every path dodot computes is <root>/<rest>, where <root> is one of three filesystem coordinates

On Linux, the second and third coordinates point at the same place. That's deliberate: a portable pack written with _app/ prefixes (§4.4) routes correctly on both platforms without per-OS branches inside the pack. On macOS the third root diverges, and the _app/ machinery starts to matter.

The dotfiles root — where dodot reads from — is a separate concern. dodot picks it from $DOTFILES_ROOT, then git rev-parse --show-toplevel, then the current directory. See.

docs.title: 3. The default rule
docs.text:
Pack-root entries — files or directories — deploy to $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/<pack>/<rel_path>. The pack name namespaces things under XDG, matching how nvim, helix, ghostty, kitty, alacritty, lazygit, starship, and most other modern tools read their configuration: from ~/.config/<tool>/.

Default rule

nvim/init.lua          ->  ~/.config/nvim/init.lua
nvim/lua/              ->  ~/.config/nvim/lua/      (whole dir, one symlink)
warp/themes/dark.yaml  ->  ~/.config/warp/themes/dark.yaml

A pack with an ordering prefix has the prefix stripped from the deployed name: 010-nvim/init.lua deploys to ~/.config/nvim/init.lua, not ~/.config/010-nvim/init.lua. The numeric prefix governs dodot up execution order, not the deployed path.

Anything not claimed by a more specific handler (shell, path, install, homebrew) flows through the symlink handler and the rules below.

docs.title: 4. Escape hatches, by what you want to do
docs.text: The cases where the default isn't right map onto specific mechanisms. Pick the one that matches your goal — they compose, but reaching for the simplest one that works keeps your packs readable.

docs.title: 4.1. One file to ~/.X (Unix canon)
docs.text:
For Unix-tradition files (`bashrc`, `zshrc`, `ssh`, …) the convention demands `$HOME/.X`. Two ways to ask for that

- *Rely on the defaults.* dodot ships a `force_home` list that auto-routes the canonical names: `ssh`, `aws`, `kube`, `bashrc`, `zshrc`, `profile`, `bash_profile`, `bash_login`, `bash_logout`, `inputrc`. A pack-root file or directory whose name matches lands at `$HOME/.<name>` without any prefix in your pack tree.

The prefix is home. (a word, not a bare .) so editors and ls don't hide your pack's source files behind dotfile-hiding. The leading . is added back on the deployed side automatically.

Top-level only — a nested subdir/home.X is treated as a literal filename and goes through the default rule.

docs.title: 4.2. A subtree to $HOME
docs.text:
If a whole directory of files belongs under `$HOME/.X` rather than `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/\<pack>/X`, use the `_home/` directory prefix

<pack>/_home/vim/vimrc      ->  ~/.vim/vimrc
<pack>/_home/.local/bin/x   ->  ~/.local/bin/x

Like every directory prefix, _home/ skips pack namespacing — the pack name doesn't appear in the deployed path.

docs.title: 4.3. A subtree to XDG without your pack name in the path
docs.text:
Sometimes a single pack holds config for several tools, and you don't want the pack name to show up at the deploy site. The `_xdg/` directory prefix solves that

term-config/_xdg/ghostty/config            ->  ~/.config/ghostty/config
term-config/_xdg/kitty/kitty.conf          ->  ~/.config/kitty/kitty.conf
term-config/_xdg/alacritty/alacritty.toml  ->  ~/.config/alacritty/alacritty.toml

The pack name (term-config) plays no role inside _xdg/. Each first-level child is the deploy directory under $XDG_CONFIG_HOME.

For a single file, xdg.X is the per-file equivalent: pack/xdg.foo.list deploys to $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/foo.list, also skipping pack namespacing.

docs.title: 4.4. A macOS GUI app
docs.text:
macOS GUI apps read configuration from `~/Library/Application Support/\<App>/`, a third coordinate beyond `$HOME` and `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME`. There are three ways to route there, in increasing order of explicitness

- *`force_app` defaults.* dodot ships a small curated list (`Code`, `Cursor`, `Zed`, `Emacs`) so a pack-root entry whose name matches deploys directly to `<app_support_dir>/<name>/...`. Matching is case-sensitive — Library folder names are case-sensitive on macOS.

The same pack tree works on both platforms — _app/ routes through app_support_dir, which is the Library path on macOS and $XDG_CONFIG_HOME on Linux.

- *`[symlink.app_aliases]` for natural pack names.* If you want a lowercase, terminal-friendly pack name (`vscode`) to deploy to a GUI-app folder name (`Code`), declare the rewrite in the root config

[symlink.app_aliases]
vscode = "Code"
warp   = "dev.warp.Warp-Stable"

With this in place, the pack vscode deploys via the default rule but to <app_support_dir>/Code/<rel_path> instead of $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/vscode/<rel_path>. The pack tree stays clean — no _app/ prefixes everywhere.

Per-file form: app.X deploys a single file to <app_support_dir>/X.

docs.title: 4.5. A macOS Library subtree that isn't Application Support
docs.text:
For `~/Library/LaunchAgents/`, `~/Library/Fonts/`, `~/Library/Services/`, etc., use `_lib/`

<pack>/_lib/LaunchAgents/com.example.foo.plist  ->  ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.example.foo.plist
<pack>/_lib/Fonts/MyFont.otf                    ->  ~/Library/Fonts/MyFont.otf

_lib/ is macOS-only. On Linux it produces a soft warning and skips the file; the rest of the pack deploys normally. The matching per-file form is lib.X for top-level files (also macOS-only, also warns and skips on Linux).

docs.title: 4.6. Anywhere else, absolute or arbitrary
docs.text:
`[symlink.targets]` is the catch-all. It maps a pack-relative source path to any destination

[symlink.targets]
"mysterious.conf" = "/etc/mysterious.conf"
"home-bound.conf" = "my-documents/home-bound.conf"

Absolute paths are used as-is. Relative paths are resolved from $XDG_CONFIG_HOME. [symlink.targets] overrides every other rule — except for the conflict case in §6.

docs.title: 5. The resolution priority, in one paragraph
docs.text:
When more than one rule could apply to a file, dodot resolves in this order: a [symlink.targets] entry wins absolutely; then file-level prefixes (home.X, app.X, xdg.X, lib.X); then directory prefixes (_home/, _xdg/, _app/, _lib/); then the force_home and force_app lists; then [symlink.app_aliases]; then the default XDG rule. Higher-priority rules skip pack namespacing; lower-priority rules apply after the pack name is in the deployed path.

For the full ladder with corner cases, see.

docs.title: 6. Watch out for
docs.text:
• Routing override conflict. Declaring a file in [symlink.targets] and giving it a routing prefix (home.X, _home/X, app.X, _app/X, …) raises a hard error rather than silently picking one. Two ways to say where one file goes is bug-bait — pick one. Other files in the pack continue to deploy.
  • Filename prefixes are top-level only. home.bashrc at the pack root deploys to ~/.bashrc. subdir/home.bashrc deploys to ~/.config/<pack>/subdir/home.bashrc literally — no rewrite. Same for app.X, xdg.X, lib.X.
  • _lib/ and lib.X are macOS-only. On Linux they warn and skip. Other entries in the same pack deploy normally.
  • Per-file mode for directories. By default, a pack-root directory is wholesale-linked: one symlink for the entire directory. Listing a file inside it in [symlink.targets], or having one match protected_paths, flips that directory into per-file mode — one symlink per file, each resolved independently.
  • Empty remainders fall through. A literal filename home. (nothing after the dot) is treated as the default rule, not as "deploy to bare $HOME/". Same for app., xdg., lib..
  • Pack ordering prefixes are stripped. 010-nvim/init.lua deploys to ~/.config/nvim/init.lua. The numeric prefix governs execution order, not the deployed path.
  • Protected paths refuse to deploy. dodot ships a default list (SSH private keys, .gnupg, AWS credentials, .kube/config, etc.) that the symlink handler refuses to touch. Override under [symlink] protected_paths if you have a justified case.

docs.title: 7. Live edits
docs.text: Once a file is symlinked, the deployed path is your source via the symlink chain — edits to either end show up at the other immediately. Whether the program reading the config picks up the change is up to the program: shells re-read on next launch, file-watching editors (nvim with autoread, vscode) reload immediately, daemons and window managers usually need an explicit reload. Adding or removing a source file in your pack still requires another dodot up to update the symlink set.

docs.title: 8. See also
docs.text:
• — the symlink handler from the handler side.
  • — full priority ladder, all edge cases, adopt inference.
  • — the inverse direction: deployed path back into a pack.
  • — macOS plist clean/smudge filters; the third-coordinate use case in detail.
  • — the [symlink] section schema.
  • . ,,

docs.title: Templates
docs.text: For the concept-level view of how preprocessing fits into dodot, see. For terminology, see.

docs.title: Template Expansion
docs.text:
Some dotfiles need to vary between machines: a different git user per host, an OS-specific Homebrew prefix, a laptop-vs-workstation shell prompt. Rather than forking configs or hand-editing after every clone, dodot renders Jinja2-style templates at deploy time. One versioned source file can produce different outputs on different hosts.

Any pack file whose name ends in .tmpl or .template is a template. dodot strips that extension, renders the content, and hands the result to the normal handler pipeline. git/gitconfig.tmpl is rendered and then symlinked as ~/.gitconfig, exactly as if gitconfig had been there all along.

Rendering is transparent: there is no dodot render step, no staging area, no "please remember to regenerate." Every dodot up re-renders, so editing the template or changing a variable picks up on the next deploy.

docs.title: 1. A First Example
docs.text:
A git config you want on every machine, with a different user per host

The .tmpl extension is the only trigger — no flags, no opt-in. dodot status shows the file under its stripped name (gitconfig, not gitconfig.tmpl), because that's what actually gets deployed.

docs.title: 2. What You Can Reference in a Template
docs.text:
Three namespaces are always available inside a template

- `dodot.*` — built-in values describing the machine and your dotfiles setup.
- `env.*` — lookup of the current process's environment variables.
- Bare names — variables you define under `[preprocessor.template.vars]`.

os, arch, home, and dotfiles_root are always populated. hostname and username are best-effort: if dodot can't detect them, the key is omitted rather than silently set to an empty string. See section 5 for how to tolerate that.

The env namespace is looked up on demand. {{ env.EDITOR }} calls the equivalent of std::env::var("EDITOR") at render time — so whatever the user has in their shell when dodot up runs is what gets baked in.

docs.title: 3. Defining Your Own Variables
docs.text:
User variables live under [preprocessor.template.vars] in any .dodot.toml — root or pack. Values are strings; you reference them by bare name.

Declaring vars in a root config

# ~/dotfiles/.dodot.toml
[preprocessor.template.vars]
editor = "nvim"
host_tier = "workstation"

Using them in a template

# ~/dotfiles/tools/aliases.sh.tmpl
alias e='{{ editor }}'
# tier: {{ host_tier }}

Pack-level vars override root-level vars of the same name. The natural workflow: set defaults in the root .dodot.toml, then override per pack when a specific pack needs something different. No merging inside a single value — override replaces.

Reserved names: dodot and env are the built-in namespaces, and cannot be used as variable names. dodot refuses to start up with a clear error if it finds dodot = "..." or env = "..." in [preprocessor.template.vars].

docs.title: 4. Branching on Host or OS
docs.text:
The common case for templates is conditional content. Jinja's `{% if %}` / `{% else %}` block handles it

Hostname-based branching works the same way

For anything more complex than OS or hostname, define your own classifier variable (e.g. host_role = "work" in the per-machine pack config) and branch on that.

docs.title: 4.1. When Not To Use A Template
docs.text:
Templates are the right tool when a file always exists but its content varies between hosts. They are the wrong tool when the question is binary — does this file deploy on this host or not?

For "deploy or don't" questions, use a gate instead of wrapping the whole file in {% if dodot.os == "..." %}. Gates skip the preprocessing pipeline entirely (no template render, no provider calls, no baseline-cache writes for files that don't apply here), and the filesystem layout shows what runs where without having to read into each .tmpl file.

Three quick examples

Gates and templates compose: aliases._darwin.sh.tmpl is a darwin-only template — the gate runs first (drops on Linux), the template renders second (only on darwin). See for the full conditional-running surface.

docs.title: 5. Undefined Variables Are Errors; Defaults Are Explicit
docs.text:
dodot runs templates in strict mode: referencing a variable that doesn't exist is a render error, and dodot up refuses to deploy that pack. This is deliberate — silently substituting an empty string on a typo is exactly how you end up with a .gitconfig that has email = @example.com and don't notice for six months.

Undefined variable surfaces clearly

$ cat ~/dotfiles/vim/gvimrc.tmpl
set background={{ theme }}

$ dodot up vim
... template render failed for ~/dotfiles/vim/gvimrc.tmpl:
      undefined value (in <string>:1)
      hint: define the variable in [preprocessor.template.vars] in .dodot.toml,
